analogy: Think of "Landforms and their Evolution" through the chapter's own opening images: CLIMATE This unit deals with • Atmosphere — compositions and structure; elements of weather and climate • Insolation — angle of incidence and distribution; heat budget of the earth — heating and cooling of atmosphere (conduction, convection, terrestrial radiation, advection); temperature — factors controlling temperature;... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Landforms and their Evolution is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that cLIMATE This unit deals with • Atmosphere — compositions and structure; elements of weather and climate • Insolation — angle of incidence and distribution; heat budget of the earth — heating and cooling of atmosphere (conduction, convection, terrestrial radiation, advection); temperature — factors controlling temperature; distribution of temperature — horizontal and vertical; inversion of temperature • Pressure — pressure belts; winds-planetary seasonal and local, air masses and fronts; tropical and extra tropical cyclones • Precipitation — evaporation; condensation — dew, frost, fog, mist and cloud; rainfall — types and world distributon • World climates — classification (Koeppen), greenhouse effect, global warming and climatic changes UNIT IV

The text explains that cOMPOSITION AND STRUCTURE OF ATMOSPHERE C an a person live without air?

The text explains that we eat food two - three times a day and drink water more frequently but breathe every few seconds.

The text explains that air is essential to the survival of all organisms.

The text explains that some organisms like humans may survive for some time without food and water but can’t survive even a few minutes without breathing air.

The text explains that that shows the reason why we should understand the atmosphere in greater detail.

The text explains that atmosphere is a mixture of different gases and it envelopes the earth all round.

The text explains that it contains life-giving gases like oxygen for humans and animals and carbon dioxide for plants.

The text explains that the air is an integral part of the earth’s mass and 99 per cent of the total mass of the atmosphere is confined to the height of 32 km from the earth’s surface.

The text explains that the air is colourless and odourless and can be felt only when it blows as wind.

The text explains that can you imagine what will happen to us in the absence of ozone in the atmosphere?

The text explains that cOMPOSITION OF THE ATMOSPHERE The atmosphere is composed of gases, water vapour and dust particles.

The text explains that the proportion of gases changes in the higher layers of the atmosphere in such a way that oxygen will be almost in negligible quantity at the height of 120 km.

The text explains that similarly, carbon dioxide and water vapour are found only up to 90 km from the surface of the earth.

The text explains that gases Carbon dioxide is meteorologically a very important gas as it is transparent to the incoming solar radiation but opaque to the outgoing terrestrial radiation.

The text explains that it absorbs a part of terrestrial radiation and reflects back some part of it towards the earth’s surface.

The text explains that this has also increased the temperature of the air.

The text explains that cOMPOSITION AND STRUCTURE OF ATMOSPHERE 65 Dust Particles Atmosphere has a sufficient capacity to keep small solid particles, which may originate from different sources and include sea salts, fine soil, smoke-soot, ash, pollen, dust and disintegrated particles of meteors.

The text explains that sTRUCTURE OF THE ATMOSPHERE The atmosphere consists of different layers with varying density and temperature.

The text explains that density is highest near the surface of the earth and decreases with increasing altitude.

The text explains that the column of atmosphere is divided into five dif ferent layers depending upon the temperature condition.

The text explains that the temperature in this layer decreases at the rate of 1°C for every 165m of height.

The text explains that the air temperature at the tropopause is about minus 800C over the equator and about minus 45oC over the poles.

The text explains that the temperature here is nearly constant, and hence, it is called the tropopause.

The text explains that in this layer, once again, temperature starts decreasing with the increase in altitude and reaches up to minus 100°C at the height of 80 km.

The text explains that radio waves transmitted from the earth are reflected back to the earth by this layer.

The text explains that temperature here starts increasing with height.

The text explains that elements of Weather and Climate The main elements of atmosphere which are subject to change and which influence human life on earth are temperature, pressure, winds, humidity, clouds and precipitation.

The text explains that (i) Which one of the following gases constitutes the major portion of the atmosphere?

The text explains that (a) Oxygen (c) Argon (b) Nitrogen (d) Carbon dioxide (ii) Atmospheric layer important for human beings is: (a) Stratosphere (c) Troposphere (b) Mesosphere (d) Ionosphere (iii) Sea salt, pollen, ash, smoke soot, fine soil — these are associated with: (a) Gases (c) Water vapour (b) Dust particles (d) Meteors (iv) Oxygen gas is in negligible quantity at the height of atmosphere: (a) 90 km (c) 100 km (b) 120 km (d) 150 km (v) Which one of the following gases is transparent to incoming solar radiation and opaque to outgoing terrestrial radiation?

The text explains that (a) Oxygen (c) Helium (b) Nitrogen (d) Carbon dioxide 2.

The text explains that (ii) What are the elements of weather and climate?

The text explains that (iii) Describe the composition of atmosphere.

The text explains that (iv) Why is troposphere the most important of all the layers of the atmosphere?

The text explains that (i) Describe the composition of the atmosphere.

The text explains that (ii) Draw a suitable diagram for the structure of the atmosphere and label it and describe it.

The text explains that these elements have been dealt in detail in Chapters 8, 9 and 10.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Fundamentals of Physical Geography, Chapter 7: Landforms and their Evolution

schemes.name: Dust

prelims_traps: Do not treat CLIMATE and COMPOSITION AND STRUCTURE as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Landforms and their Evolution; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Landforms and their Evolution through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use CLIMATE and COMPOSITION AND STRUCTURE to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
